# Building a Local Wispr Flow Clone on Windows 11 + AMD RX 6800 XT

**A research and build-plan report for an experienced .NET/C# and Python developer**

Target hardware: all-AMD, Windows 11, Radeon RX 6800 XT (16 GB VRAM, gfx1030 / RDNA2), no CUDA, with a CPU-only degrade path.

> **One correction up front, since it shapes the whole architecture:** your instinct that "a local model on Ollama" could do the whole thing is half right. Ollama does the *text* half beautifully — it's exactly the right tool for the cleanup/formatting "magic." But **Ollama has no audio input at all** (it's text/image-in, text-out; the audio feature is an open, unimplemented request — ollama/ollama#11798). The "whisper" tags you may see on ollama.com are unofficial uploads that do not actually run ASR in Ollama's engine. So speech-to-text must be a **separate upstream component** (a Whisper-family model via whisper.cpp or faster-whisper), and Ollama sits *after* it. Every serious local tool in this space (whisper-local, FreeFlow, superwhisper) uses exactly this two-process split. This is not a limitation you can engineer around — it's the fundamental shape of the system.

---

## A. How Wispr Flow actually works

Wispr Flow is a **cloud-only, two-stage pipeline** wearing the UI of a system-wide dictation tool. Understanding the real architecture tells you exactly which parts to replicate locally and which parts are cloud-scale luxuries you can skip.

### The end-to-end pipeline

```
[Hold hotkey] → [capture mic audio on-device]
   → [upload audio over TLS to Wispr cloud (AWS, served on Baseten)]
      → Stage 1: ASR — proprietary speech-recognition model(s) → raw transcript
      → Stage 2: fine-tuned Meta Llama → cleanup/format/tone-match → final text
   → [return text to client]
      → [inject at cursor in the focused app (clipboard-paste primary, keystroke/manual fallback)]
```

Wispr's own engineering post budgets this as ASR < 200 ms, LLM < 200 ms, networking ≤ 200 ms, with the whole thing under **700 ms at p99** (not p50 — that precision is confirmed verbatim in Baseten's case study).

### Each "magic" feature, tagged cloud-vs-local and confirmed-vs-inferred

| Feature | What it does | Cloud or local? | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Push-to-talk → auto-insert at cursor** | Hold key, speak, formatted text lands at cursor in any app | Capture local; transcription cloud; insertion local | **Confirmed** (primary docs) |
| **AI Auto-Edits / Smart Formatting** | Removes fillers (um/uh), auto-punctuates, capitalizes, formats lists, handles self-corrections ("2… actually 3") | **Cloud** (fine-tuned Llama) | **Confirmed** it's a post-ASR *layer* (docs: turning it off "pastes raw transcription") |
| **Per-app tone matching ("Styles")** | Formal in email, casual in Slack; app categorized into Email/Work/Personal/Other | **Cloud** (Llama, fed active-app identity) | **Confirmed** |
| **Context Awareness** | Reads limited text near cursor + active-app/website identity + app metadata + (in IDEs) variable/file names + recent chat messages; uploads to cloud to spell proper nouns right | **Cloud** (this is the privacy-sensitive part) | **Confirmed**; password fields excluded; **screenshot OCR is opt-in** (default off) after a 2025 backlash |
| **Custom Dictionary + auto-learn** | User-editable term list; learns from your typed corrections (opt-in setting) | Cloud-synced | **Confirmed** (auto-learn is opt-in, not default-on) |
| **Command Mode** | Highlight text, speak an instruction ("make this concise", "translate to Polish"), AI rewrites in place | **Cloud**; paid-gated (Pro *or* active trial) | **Confirmed** ("Pro-only" is slightly overstated — trial users get it) |
| **Whisper mode** | Dictate quietly; no toggle | Cloud | Confirmed |
| **100+ languages, code-switching** | Auto-detect; mixed-language | Cloud | Languages **confirmed**; **code-switching is experimental/limited** — docs warn against sentence-by-sentence switching |

### What's confirmed vs. inferred about the internals (this matters for the clone)

- **CONFIRMED:** Transcription *always* occurs in the cloud — Wispr's exact words. There is no offline mode at any tier. The backend must decrypt audio to transcribe (no true end-to-end encryption).
- **CONFIRMED:** Stage 2 is **fine-tuned Meta Llama** for transcript enhancement (Baseten primary source). Third-party providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cerebras) also appear — from a *publicly published* subprocessor list, not (as one finder claimed) an NDA-gated one.
- **REFUTED / OVERSTATED:** The "Llama **3.1**" version is unsupported fabricated specificity — every primary source says "Llama" with no version. The "voice-first" and "custom ASR" labels are marketing/inference, not primary-sourced. The ASR being **Whisper-derived is not confirmed** — it's a plausible inference only because Wispr's serving vendor (Baseten) publicly runs an optimized streaming Whisper, but Baseten's public Whisper customer is Notion, not Wispr. **Treat "Wispr uses Whisper" as an open question, not a fact.**
- **Privacy default worth noting:** for ordinary consumer accounts, retention + training-use are **ON by default**; zero-retention requires enabling Privacy Mode *and* disabling Cloud Sync.

**The takeaway for your clone:** you are replicating the *user-visible behavior* (hotkey → clean formatted text at cursor) and the *architecture* (STT → LLM polish → inject), but keeping everything **on-device** — which is precisely the privacy gap Wispr leaves open. You do **not** need cloud-scale streaming ASR, a router model, or multi-provider LLM fan-out for the MVP.

---

## B. Reference architecture for a local clone

Here is the component pipeline as a diagram-in-text. Each box is a swappable component; the arrows are the data contract between them.

**Key data contracts:**

1. **Hotkey → capture:** key-down starts recording, key-up stops it. This is why `RegisterHotKey` alone is insufficient — it fires only on key-down and *cannot report key-up*. You need a `WH_KEYBOARD_LL` low-level hook (or `GetAsyncKeyState` polling) for true hold-to-talk.
2. **Capture → VAD → ASR:** 16 kHz mono PCM; Silero VAD trims silence so you only decode speech (Silero is ~4× more accurate than webrtcvad at 5% FPR and is what makes it feel responsive).
3. **ASR → cleanup:** raw transcript string. **Guard: skip the LLM pass for very short snippets (< ~50 chars)** — small models mangle tiny inputs, and whisper-local ships exactly this guard.
4. **Active-window context → cleanup:** the foreground process name (`GetForegroundWindow → GetWindowThreadProcessId → process name`) is fed into the LLM prompt to bias tone. *For MVP this is optional* — it's the seed of per-app context, which is a later phase.
5. **Cleanup → inject:** cleaned text. **Dual injection strategy** (see §D): clipboard-paste default, Unicode `SendInput` fallback, always leave text on the clipboard so dictation is never lost.

---

## C. Concrete local stack FOR THIS AMD BOX

This is the opinionated part. Below is a specific pick for each component that **actually runs on the RX 6800 XT**, plus the CPU-only fallback, plus the language recommendation.

### The critical AMD constraint you must design around

**faster-whisper / CTranslate2 has NO working AMD-GPU path on Windows.** This is confirmed, not speculative:
- CTranslate2's GPU backend is **CUDA-only**; ROCm is community wheels, not a primary backend.
- The community ROCm wheels are **broken on Windows**: built against ROCm 7.2, but the Windows HIP SDK only reaches 7.1.1, and a `hipblas.dll` rename crashes CTranslate2 on load.
- Worse for *this specific card*: ROCm/HIP on Windows officially supports only **RDNA3/RDNA4**. The RX 6800 XT is **gfx1030 / RDNA2 — not on AMD's official Windows ROCm list.** (There's even a bug where ROCm misdetects it as gfx1036.)

So on this box, faster-whisper is your **CPU** engine, not your GPU engine. For GPU ASR you go through **Vulkan** (vendor-agnostic, needs only the Adrenalin driver) or DirectCompute.

> **Important nuance — ASR vs. LLM take different AMD paths.** The gfx1030 GPU-support story is *different* for the two stages:
> - **ASR (whisper.cpp):** ROCm is not viable → use **Vulkan**.
> - **LLM (Ollama):** gfx1030 **IS** officially supported by Ollama — it's in Ollama's ROCm LLVM-target list — *and* Ollama ships a bundled Vulkan backend on Windows. So Ollama has two working paths here where whisper.cpp effectively has one.

### Recommended stack (GPU path)

| Component | Pick | Model | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Language / app shell** | **Python** | — | ASR + LLM tooling is Python-native; see justification below |
| **Mic capture + VAD + streaming** | **RealtimeSTT** (MIT) or roll your own with **sounddevice** | — | RealtimeSTT bundles 16 kHz capture + WebRTC/Silero VAD + faster-whisper; `sounddevice` gives NumPy callbacks if you build capture yourself |
| **VAD** | **Silero VAD** | — | ~4× fewer errors than webrtcvad; built into faster-whisper (`vad_filter=True`) |
| **ASR (GPU)** | **whisper.cpp, Vulkan build** (`-DGGML_VULKAN=1`) | **`large-v3-turbo`** (GGML, ~6 GB — trivial in 16 GB) or `small`/`medium` for lowest latency | Vulkan runs ~as fast as CUDA, ~8–12× realtime on AMD; turbo is ~6–7× faster than large-v3 at near-large accuracy (809M params, 4 decoder layers) |
| **LLM cleanup** | **Ollama** (v0.12.11+) | **`qwen2.5:7b`** (sweet spot); `qwen2.5:3b` / `gemma3:4b` for speed; `qwen2.5:14b` Q4 for max quality | Ollama's gfx1030 support is official; Qwen2.5-7B beats Llama-3.1-8B/Gemma2-9B on instruction-following; ~60–76 tok/s on 7B Q4 here |
| **Text injection** | **Direct ctypes `SendInput`** + clipboard-paste | — | Dual strategy; don't use pynput/pyautogui/SendKeys wrappers (they get Unicode/surrogate handling wrong) |
| **Hotkey** | `keyboard` lib or direct `WH_KEYBOARD_LL` | — | Need key-up for hold-to-talk |
| **Tray + settings** | **pystray** (or PySide/Qt for a richer settings window) | — | Solved problem |

**How to get a Vulkan whisper.cpp binary without fighting a source build:** official whisper.cpp Windows releases ship CPU + CUDA only — **no Vulkan**. Three ways around it, in order of least friction:
1. **Lemonade SDK's `lemonade-sdk/whisper.cpp-amd`** — self-contained archives with Vulkan runtime bundled, auto-synced to upstream within 24 h. (Its *ROCm* builds are RDNA3/4-only and won't help you, but the *Vulkan* build in the same release is architecture-agnostic and covers the 6800 XT.)
2. Prebuilt Vulkan binaries: `jerryshell/whisper.cpp-windows-vulkan-bin` or `DomoticX/whisper.cpp-windows-vulkan`.
3. Compile from source with `-DGGML_VULKAN=1` + the free Vulkan SDK (mirror how **Vibe** does it).

**Alternative GPU engine if Vulkan misbehaves:** **Const-me/Whisper** (WhisperDesktop, MPL-2.0) is a **DirectCompute / Direct3D 11** engine that runs on *any* DX11 GPU including Radeon, with **zero** CUDA/ROCm/Vulkan dependency and a 431 KB DLL. It has a built-in live-mic capture screen — excellent for *validating* the 6800 XT before you build anything. MPL-2.0 is file-level copyleft, so you can wrap its DLL in a proprietary/MIT app if you keep the MPL files separate.

**Do NOT use for real-time on this box:** the DirectML (`onnxruntime-directml`) path works on AMD without ROCm and is fast (~8–12× realtime) but is **batch/file-only, explicitly not streaming** — fine as a bulk transcriber, wrong for live dictation. Also skip WhisperX and WhisperS2T (CUDA-centric, batch-oriented; WhisperX rides CTranslate2 and inherits the no-AMD-GPU problem).

### CPU-only fallback (the degrade path)

| Component | Pick | Model |
|---|---|---|
| **ASR** | **faster-whisper** (`pip install faster-whisper`) | `base` or `small`, `compute_type="int8"` |
| **LLM** | **Ollama** auto-offloads to RAM | `qwen2.5:3b` / `gemma3:4b` / `phi-4-mini` at Q4 |

This is the **simplest, most stable install** — no GPU backend headaches at all. faster-whisper CPU int8 runs ~15–20× realtime for tiny/base and hits ~200 ms latency / 3.4% WER for `small` — comfortably real-time for dictation. Ollama CPU is 5–20× slower (~3–15 tok/s on 7B), so drop to a 3–4B model there. Use this path as the guaranteed-works baseline and as the automatic fallback when Vulkan flakes.

### Language recommendation: **build it in Python**

**Recommendation: Python for the whole app.** The reasoning is decisive and specific to this project:

- **The core value (ASR + LLM) is Python-native.** RealtimeSTT, faster-whisper, whisper.cpp bindings, Silero VAD, and every reference clone (whisper-local, whisper-writer, FreeFlow) live in Python. Building the app in C#/WPF or Electron forces a **fragile cross-process Python sidecar** for ASR — extra process, IPC, and packaging complexity for **zero integration gain**.
- **The Windows-integration half is thin.** Hotkey, mic, injection, active-window, tray — all reduce to a handful of Win32 APIs that Python P/Invokes via `ctypes`/`pywin32` **as capably as C#**. There is no Windows capability C# has here that Python lacks.

Given your stated strength in .NET/C# WPF, here's the honest tradeoff: **C# WPF is a legitimate runner-up, but only if you accept running Python ASR as a spawned sidecar** (stdio or local socket). Its integration story is actually the *cleanest* of the three stacks — NHotkey for hotkeys, Hardcodet/H.NotifyIcon for the tray, native `SendInput`. Choose WPF *only* if a single native EXE and a polished WPF settings UI matter more to you than avoiding the sidecar. **Reject Electron outright** — its `globalShortcut` can't cleanly do PTT key-up, native injection depends on the packaging-fragile robotjs/nut.js, and it *still* needs the Python ASR sidecar. You'd get the worst of every option.

My call: **Python.** Your C# skills transfer to the P/Invoke layer conceptually, and you skip the sidecar entirely.

---

## D. The hard parts / risks

Ordered by how much they'll bite you.

### 1. Text injection — the single biggest reliability pitfall

No single injection method is universal. Production apps converge on a **dual strategy**, and you should too:

- **Default: clipboard-set-then-paste.** Save current clipboard → set transcribed text → synthesize Ctrl+V (Shift+Insert for terminals/IDEs) → restore prior clipboard. Fast, format-preserving. This is Wispr's *primary* desktop mechanism (a verdict corrected the earlier "Accessibility API insertion" framing — desktop is clipboard-first, not AX-API-first). Risks: a save/restore **race window**, and it breaks where clipboard redirection is disabled (RDP/Citrix, locked-down enterprise).
- **Fallback: direct `SendInput` with `KEYEVENTF_UNICODE`.** Works over RDP and where clipboard is blocked; layout-proof (sends Unicode code points, immune to non-QWERTY layouts). Risks: (a) **UIPI blocks it entirely when the foreground app is elevated/admin** — a non-elevated app simply *cannot* type into an elevated window (the only sanctioned bypass is a signed, Program-Files-installed, `UIAccess=true`-manifested app); (b) characters above the BMP (emoji, rare CJK) **must be split into high+low UTF-16 surrogate pairs** sent as two INPUT events, or they corrupt; (c) character-by-character, so slow.
- **Always leave the text on the clipboard** so a failed insert never loses dictation. Offer manual-paste as the last resort.
- **Write this layer with direct ctypes `SendInput`, not pynput/pyautogui/SendKeys** — those wrappers get surrogate-pair and layout handling wrong. `System.Windows.Forms.SendKeys` (if you ever go C#) is Microsoft-discouraged for international use — avoid.

**Document the UIPI limitation to the user up front.** It's a Windows constraint, not a bug you can fix without code-signing.

### 2. AMD-on-Windows GPU quirks

- **ASR:** do **not** invest in ROCm for whisper on this gfx1030 card — it's not officially supported on Windows and the CTranslate2 wheels are broken. Use **Vulkan** (or Const-me DirectCompute). Install the latest Adrenalin driver + Vulkan SDK.
- **Ollama:** install **v0.12.11 or newer** — versions v0.12.1–v0.12.10 had a gfx1030 crash regression (access violation `0xc0000005` on ROCm init, issue #12388). Try native ROCm first (should "just work" for gfx1030 on a current version, no `HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION` needed); if the ROCm backend won't load, fall back to the bundled **Vulkan** path (`OLLAMA_VULKAN=1`, pick the device with `GGML_VK_VISIBLE_DEVICES`). Verify with `ollama ps` (should show 100% GPU). **Do NOT install the `likelovewant/ollama-for-amd` fork** — it's for older/unsupported archs; mainline covers your card.

### 3. Latency budget

Wispr's cloud does the whole thing in ~700 ms p99. Locally, your budget looks like:
- **ASR:** ~0.5–2 s behind live speech for a sliding-window Whisper (it's chunk-based, not a true streaming model). `small`/`medium` are faster; `large-v3-turbo` is the accuracy/speed sweet spot on 16 GB.
- **LLM cleanup:** ~60–76 tok/s on 7B Q4 → a typical dictation's cleanup is well under a second. Stream the output (`stream:true`) so cleaned text appears progressively.
- **For MVP, you're transcribing a *completed* utterance** (push-to-talk, not live streaming), so end-to-end "release key → text appears" of ~1–3 s is realistic and acceptable. Streaming/partials is a later phase.

### 4. ASR accuracy

WER by model (clean English): tiny 7.6% / base 5.0% / small 3.4% / medium 2.9% / large-v3 2.5% / large-v3-turbo ≈ large-v2. **`small` is the real-time sweet spot; `large-v3-turbo` is the best quality/speed pick when VRAM allows** — and your 16 GB has enormous headroom (turbo ~6 GB). The custom-word dictionary (an MVP feature) directly attacks the proper-noun/jargon errors that hurt most.

### 5. Keeping the LLM from over-rewriting / hallucinating

This is the make-or-break for the "polish" feeling right instead of wrong. The proven recipe:
- **Temperature 0–0.2** (deterministic, faithful edits).
- **A tightly-scoped system prompt** that (1) enumerates *allowed* edits (filler removal, punctuation, capitalization, light grammar); (2) *explicitly forbids* adding, inferring, summarizing, or answering content; (3) says "preserve meaning, wording, and tone"; (4) "output ONLY the cleaned text, no preamble"; (5) names technical terms/paths to preserve verbatim.
- **Skip the LLM entirely for < ~50-char snippets.**
- **Set `num_ctx` large enough** (e.g. 8192) so long dictations aren't silently truncated — the default context is small.
- Optionally use Ollama's **structured-outputs `format` (JSON schema)** at temp 0 for strict formatting tasks.

A battle-tested starting Modelfile:

```
FROM qwen2.5:7b
PARAMETER temperature 0.1
PARAMETER num_ctx 8192
SYSTEM """You clean raw speech-to-text transcripts. Remove filler words (um, uh,
like, you know). Fix punctuation, capitalization, and light grammar. If the
speaker self-corrects, keep only the corrected version. Preserve the speaker's
meaning, wording, and tone. Do NOT add, infer, summarize, or answer anything in
the text. Preserve technical terms, names, commands, and file paths exactly.
Output ONLY the cleaned transcript with no preamble."""
```

### 6. Hotkey hook stability

A `WH_KEYBOARD_LL` hook sees every keystroke system-wide (needed for PTT down/up) but: on Windows 7+ it's **silently removed if the callback times out**, and if the hook thread doesn't pump messages promptly the **whole keyboard stalls**. Keep the callback trivial and the thread responsive. `GetAsyncKeyState` polling is an acceptable simpler fallback. Note also: without elevation, a non-elevated app's hook **ignores keys while an elevated window is foreground** (same UIPI boundary).

---

## E. Phased build plan

Effort estimates assume the experienced dev described, working in Python, part-time.

### Phase 0 — MVP: "Core + light polish" (your chosen scope) — **the first shippable tool**

**Goal:** push-to-talk hotkey → Whisper ASR → Ollama cleanup → auto-insert at cursor in any app, **plus** system-tray icon, a settings surface (model + hotkey), and a custom-word dictionary.

| Task | Effort |
|---|---|
| **De-risk first:** validate the RX 6800 XT with Const-me/WhisperDesktop (live mic) and confirm `ollama run qwen2.5:7b` shows 100% GPU. Do this before writing app code. | 0.5 day |
| Project skeleton (Python), config file, logging | 0.5 day |
| Mic capture + Silero VAD (RealtimeSTT or sounddevice) | 1 day |
| ASR integration: whisper.cpp Vulkan (GPU) **with** faster-whisper-CPU fallback behind one interface | 2–3 days |
| PTT hotkey via `WH_KEYBOARD_LL` (key-down start / key-up stop) | 1–2 days |
| Ollama cleanup call (streaming, temp 0.1, the system prompt above, < 50-char skip guard) | 1 day |
| **Text injection dual strategy** (clipboard-paste default + ctypes Unicode `SendInput` fallback + surrogate handling + always-leave-on-clipboard) — *budget generously, this is the hard part* | 2–3 days |
| Tray icon (pystray) + settings window (model picker, hotkey capture) | 2 days |
| Custom-word dictionary (user-editable list injected into the LLM prompt and/or Whisper `initial_prompt`) | 1 day |
| Packaging, first-run GPU/Ollama detection, error surfaces | 1–2 days |

**Phase 0 total: ~2–3 weeks** part-time. Ship this. It is a genuinely useful, fully-local Wispr Flow replacement for the core loop.

> **Steal the reference:** **`drajb/whisper-local`** (MIT, Python, Windows-first) is the single closest existing clone — it already ships SendInput injection, optional Ollama polish (off by default), a voice-command config, *and documented AMD GPU setup*. Read it end-to-end before Phase 0. Its ASR is faster-whisper (so its AMD path is the broken-on-Windows ROCm one — you'll swap in whisper.cpp-Vulkan instead), but its injection code, prompt patterns, and config model port straight over. **`cjpais/Handy`** (MIT, Rust) is the best-architected skeleton if you ever reconsider language.

### Phase 1 — Streaming / partial results

Move from "transcribe completed utterance" to rolling partials (whisper-stream `--stream`, sliding-window re-decode gated by VAD, ~0.5–2 s live latency). Show interim text in a small status window. **~1 week.**

### Phase 2 — Per-app context / tone matching

Wire `GetForegroundWindow → PID → process name` into an `app_rules.yaml`, feed the active app name + a limited amount of nearby text into the LLM prompt to bias tone (formal email vs. casual chat). This is the local, privacy-preserving version of Wispr's Context Awareness — *keep the context on-device*. **~1 week** (more if you add on-screen text reading via UI Automation).

### Phase 3 — Voice commands + Command Mode

Command grammar (`commands.yaml`: trigger → run/hotkey/type/rephrase), plus a "highlight text → speak instruction → rewrite in place" mode (read selection, LLM-transform, inject). **~1–2 weeks.**

### Phase 4 — Polish

Snippets/voice shortcuts, whisper (quiet-speech) tuning, auto-learn from corrections, model auto-download UI, latency optimization, optional `UIAccess`-manifested signed build for typing into elevated windows. **Ongoing.**

---

## F. Open questions to resolve before building

These change the design materially — settle them first.

1. **Push-to-talk vs. always-listening as the default trigger?** MVP scope says PTT (hold-to-talk), which is simpler and more private (no always-on mic). Confirm PTT is the default and whether a toggle/hands-free mode is even wanted later. This decides whether you need a wake-word engine at all.

2. **Which hotkey?** Wispr defaults to Ctrl+Win on Windows. What key(s) do you want, and must it be *held* (true PTT, requiring the low-level hook) or *press-to-start / press-to-stop* (toggle, simpler)? A held key that's also a normal modifier (Ctrl) complicates the hook logic.

3. **Hard-require the GPU, or auto-degrade to CPU?** Recommendation is auto-degrade (ship both ASR engines behind one interface), but confirm — it roughly doubles the ASR integration work versus a GPU-only build. Given this is *your* box with a known-good 6800 XT, you *could* skip CPU fallback for a v0 and add it later.

4. **ASR model default:** `large-v3-turbo` (best quality, ~6 GB, ~0.5–2 s latency) or `small` (lowest latency, 3.4% WER)? Both fit trivially. This is a latency-vs-accuracy preference call.

5. **LLM model default and cleanup aggressiveness:** `qwen2.5:7b` (sweet spot) vs. `qwen2.5:3b`/`gemma3:4b` (faster)? And how aggressive should "polish" be — pure disfluency removal (safest, least risk of altering meaning) vs. light rephrasing? The more aggressive, the higher the over-rewrite/hallucination risk.

6. **whisper.cpp-Vulkan vs. Const-me DirectCompute for the GPU engine?** Vulkan (via a prebuilt Lemonade/jerryshell binary) integrates cleanly into a Python pipeline and supports streaming later; Const-me is a turnkey DX11 engine with a live-mic GUI but is a heavier thing to embed. Recommendation: prototype with Const-me to *validate the card this week*, build the app on whisper.cpp-Vulkan. Confirm you're OK with that.

7. **Do you want per-app context (reading nearby on-screen text) at all,** given it's the exact feature that caused Wispr's 2025 privacy backlash? Locally it never leaves the machine, but it still means the app reads screen content. Confirm it belongs in the roadmap (Phase 2) or is out of scope entirely.

8. **Typing into elevated/admin windows** — is that a requirement? If yes, you'll eventually need a code-signed, Program-Files-installed, `UIAccess=true`-manifested build (non-trivial). If no, document the clipboard-with-manual-paste fallback for those windows and move on.

9. **Language:** confirm **Python** (my recommendation) vs. **C# WPF + Python ASR sidecar** (viable given your WPF strength, but adds a sidecar). This is the biggest structural decision and everything downstream depends on it.

---

**Bottom line:** The architecture is settled and low-risk — a Python app doing `PTT hook → mic → Silero VAD → whisper.cpp-Vulkan (large-v3-turbo) → Ollama (qwen2.5:7b, temp 0.1, constrained prompt) → dual-strategy text injection`, with a faster-whisper-CPU + smaller-model degrade path. The two things that will actually consume your debugging time are **text injection reliability** (UIPI, surrogates, clipboard races) and **getting a Vulkan whisper.cpp binary running on the gfx1030 card** (sidestepped by prebuilt Lemonade/jerryshell binaries or Const-me DirectCompute). Read `drajb/whisper-local` before you start. Resolve the nine questions above, and this becomes a straightforward 2–3 week MVP.

---

**Relevant reference repositories (all URLs from finder sources):**
- `https://github.com/drajb/whisper-local` — closest existing clone; read first
- `https://github.com/cjpais/Handy` — best-architected skeleton (Rust)
- `https://github.com/Const-me/Whisper` — DirectCompute engine, validate the card
- `https://github.com/lemonade-sdk/whisper.cpp-amd` — prebuilt Vulkan whisper.cpp
- `https://github.com/thewh1teagle/vibe` — reference Vulkan build config
- `https://github.com/KoljaB/RealtimeSTT` — capture+VAD+transcription library
- `https://github.com/ggml-org/whisper.cpp` — upstream (build `-DGGML_VULKAN=1`)

verdicts.claim: Wispr Flow is a system-wide AI dictation app: push-to-talk hotkey → speak → cleaned, formatted text auto-inserts at the cursor in any focused app.
verdicts.note:
Every component confirmed by primary sources (official site + Wispr Flow Help Center docs). (1) System-wide: homepage states "Seamless speech-to-text in every application on your phone or computer" and Flow works in Gmail, Notion, Slack, VS Code, web forms, etc. [https://wisprflow.ai/]. (2) Push-to-talk hotkey: official docs give default push-to-talk keys (Mac Fn or Ctrl+Opt; Windows Ctrl+Win) with the wording "Push to talk: Hold a key to dictate" — hold, speak, release [https://docs.wisprflow.ai/articles/2612050838-supported-unsupported-keyboard-hotkey-shortcuts]. (3) Cleaned/formatted: "AI Auto Edits... Rambled thoughts become clear, perfectly formatted text, without the filler words or typos"; Smart Formatting handles punctuation, capitalization, list creation, and disfluency cleanup [https://wisprflow.ai/, https://docs.wisprflow.ai/articles/5373093536-how-do-i-use-smart-formatting-and-backtrack]. (4) Auto-insert at cursor: docs describe Flow pasting the formatted text into the focused text field at the cursor position in whatever app is active [https://docs.wisprflow.ai/].

CONFIRMED FACTS vs INFERENCE: All four mechanistic claims are directly stated by Wispr Flow. Two adversarial caveats that do NOT refute the claim: (a) the AI cleanup runs on cloud-based models, not fully on-device/local — relevant to the broader 'local AMD dictation tech' framing but the claim itself never asserts local processing; (b) Smart Formatting explicitly "does not attempt to correct misheard words," so cleanup means disfluency/grammar/format, not transcription-accuracy fixes — again not something the claim overstates. Note the claim is entirely about Wispr Flow; it makes no assertion about AMD, so nothing AMD-related bears on its truth value. Verdict: supported.

verdicts.claim: Wispr Flow's edge over OS built-in dictation is a post-transcription AI layer: filler-word removal, auto-punctuation, self-correction handling, list formatting, and per-app tone matching — versus the raw, error-prone transcription of Apple/Windows dictation.
verdicts.note:
CONFIRMED FACTS (primary sources). All five named features are documented verbatim on Wispr Flow's own official pages. Filler removal: "Flow automatically removes 'um,' 'uh,' and other pauses." Auto-punctuation: "Flow detects punctuation naturally from your pauses and tone." Self-correction: "Say 'Let's meet at 2… actually 3,' and Flow writes the corrected version instantly" (their Backtrack feature). List formatting: "Just speak the numbers... Flow turns it into a formatted list." Per-app tone matching: "Go from formal in docs, to casual in messages, to enthusiastic in emails." The "post-transcription AI layer" architecture is directly confirmed by Wispr's help docs, which state that when Smart Formatting is off, "Flow pastes your raw transcription without AI formatting" — proving the formatting is an applied layer on top of raw ASR output, not the transcription itself. An independent review (spokenly.app) corroborates all five features and frames the edge as a "cloud pipeline plus AI cleanup."

INFERENCE / SUPPORTED CHARACTERIZATION. The "raw, error-prone Apple dictation" contrast is accurate: multiple sources (including Wispr's comparison page and independent reviews) describe Apple Dictation as giving raw transcription with minimal formatting, inconsistent accuracy, and accent struggles.

ONE NUANCE (why not a blanket 'supported' without caveat, but not enough to lower the verdict). The "Windows dictation" half is slightly dated as a universal statement. Standard Windows Voice Typing IS raw — no filler removal, requires spoken 'comma'/'period'. BUT Microsoft now ships Fluid Dictation on Copilot+ PCs (NPU-equipped) that itself does auto-punctuation, grammar fixing, and filler-word removal — a directly overlapping AI layer (moremax.net; snailtext.app). So on modern Copilot+ hardware the Windows side is no longer purely 'raw.' This narrows but does not refute the claim, since (a) Fluid Dictation is gated behind Copilot+ hardware and off by default for most users, (b) it lacks per-app tone matching and cross-platform parity, and (c) the claim's core assertion — that Wispr Flow's differentiator is this AI post-processing layer — holds firmly. Verdict: supported, with the caveat that 'raw Windows dictation' is becoming less universally true as Fluid Dictation rolls out.

verdicts.claim: Context Awareness reads on-screen text, proper nouns, app metadata, and (in code editors) variable/file names to boost accuracy and spell names correctly — i.e., it is aware of the active app.
verdicts.note:
CONFIRMED by primary source (Wispr Flow's own Help Center). The Context Awareness doc states verbatim that during active dictation it sends "relevant context (nearby text, proper nouns extracted from on-screen content, app metadata, and — in coding environments — variable names and file names)" to improve transcription accuracy. It also confirms active-app awareness: the system "identifies the app you're using and adjusts your dictation accordingly," recognizing app categories (Email, Work messaging, Personal messaging, Other). Name-spelling is confirmed: "Flow uses names and context visible on screen (such as email recipients) to recognize proper nouns and preserve correct capitalization." All four claimed data types plus active-app awareness and the accuracy/name-spelling purpose are directly stated. Adversarial attempt to refute failed — no contradiction found; this is Wispr's advertised behavior.

CONFIRMED FACTS (per official docs): (1) reads nearby on-screen text + proper nouns; (2) reads app metadata; (3) in coding environments reads variable/file names; (4) detects the active app and adapts; (5) purpose is accuracy + correct proper-noun capitalization.

INFERENCE / NUANCE (not a defect in the claim): Wispr implements this as two related but distinct mechanisms — general on-screen context capture, and a separate IDE 'Variable Recognition' feature that reads code symbols directly from the active editor for a supported language set (JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, Swift, C++, C, Rust, Go); File Tagging is limited to Cursor/Windsurf while VS Code supports code-context reading but not file tagging. So 'reads on-screen text' oversimplifies the code-editor path, but does not make the claim wrong. Note: the claim/feature concerns Wispr's CLOUD dictation (screenshots/context sent to Wispr servers); it is not evidence of a local/offline or AMD-specific dictation capability.

verdicts.claim: Wispr Flow headline features: AI Auto-Edits, Whisper mode (quiet speech), Personal + shared Dictionary, Snippets, Styles/tone matching, Pro-only Command Mode for voice-editing highlighted text, 100+ languages with code-switching.
verdicts.note:
Every feature is confirmed by Wispr Flow's own primary sources; two minor wording imprecisions keep it from being flawless but nothing is refuted.

CONFIRMED FACTS (all from wisprflow.ai / docs.wisprflow.ai):
- AI Auto-Edits: homepage names the feature "AI Auto Edits" ("Speak naturally and Flow transcribes and edits your voice, instantly"). https://wisprflow.ai/
- Whisper mode: /features says "Flow even works when you're whispering, so you can keep dictating in shared, quiet spaces." https://wisprflow.ai/features
- Personal dictionary: "When you correct a spelling, Flow adds it automatically to your personal dictionary." Shared dictionary: "Add your team's names, jargon, product terms, and acronyms to a shared dictionary." Both on https://wisprflow.ai/features
- Snippets: "Create voice shortcuts for things you say often, like scheduling links, intros, or FAQs." https://wisprflow.ai/features
- Styles/tone matching: "Go from formal in docs, to casual in messages, to enthusiastic in emails." https://wisprflow.ai/features
- Command Mode voice-edits highlighted text: docs — "Highlight text in any app... Speak your command... Flow replaces the highlighted text with the result." https://docs.wisprflow.ai/articles/4816967992-how-to-use-command-mode
- 100+ languages: /features — "Flow works in 100+ languages." https://wisprflow.ai/features
- Code-switching: multi-language docs describe occasional cross-language words and a dedicated Hinglish (Hindi+English) model. https://docs.wisprflow.ai/articles/3191899797-use-flow-with-multiple-languages

TWO IMPRECISIONS (do not refute the claim):
1) "Pro-only" Command Mode — docs actually say it "requires a paid subscription OR active free trial." So it is a paid/gated feature (not on the free tier), but a trial user who has not yet bought Pro can use it. "Pro-only" is directionally right (paid-gated) but slightly overstated. Third-party reviews (spokenly.app) do list Command Mode under Pro.
2) "Code-switching" is real but LIMITED — docs explicitly warn "Flow does not support rapid language switching within a single sentence" and "works best when you speak primarily in one language with occasional words from another." Chinese+English mixing is called out as weak. So code-switching exists (esp. via the Hinglish model) but is constrained, not full sentence-by-sentence switching.

INFERENCE: Both nuances are qualifications on otherwise-correct claims, not falsifications, so the composite claim rates 'supported.' Attempts to refute (checking whether Auto-Edits is a real named feature, whether Command Mode edits selected text, whether the Pro gating and code-switching claims hold) all failed to break it.

verdicts.claim: Platforms: macOS, Windows, iOS, and Android (all four).
verdicts.note: CONFIRMED FACT: Wispr Flow's official system-requirements docs explicitly list "Available on: Mac, Windows, iOS, Android" (macOS 12+, Windows 10/11 x64, iOS 18.3+, Android 13-16). Each platform independently verified: Mac/Windows shipped first; iOS live on App Store (v1.63, June 2026); Android launched Feb 23, 2026, confirmed by independent TechCrunch reporting and Wispr's own docs, now live on Google Play (free at launch). All four platforms are genuinely shipping, so the claim holds. ADVERSARIAL CHECK / caveats (not enough to lower the verdict): (1) The main marketing download page wisprflow.ai/downloads still displays "Join waitlist for android" — a stale page contradicted by every other primary source; treat as an un-updated marketing page, not evidence Android is unavailable. (2) INFERENCE-level nuance the claim glosses over: "iOS" support is iPhone-only — iPad is explicitly NOT supported, and Linux/Chromebooks/Windows-on-ARM are also excluded. The claim says only "iOS," which is accurate, but a reader should not infer full iPadOS/tablet coverage. Verdict remains 'supported' because all four named platforms exist as real, downloadable products.

verdicts.claim: Pricing: free Flow Basic (weekly word caps); Flow Pro $15/mo or $12/mo billed annually ($144/yr); Flow Enterprise contact sales; 14-day Pro trial, no card.
verdicts.note: Every element confirmed by the official pricing page (primary source), and corroborated by the Wispr Flow Help Center docs. CONFIRMED FACTS from wisprflow.ai/pricing: (1) Free tier is named "Flow Basic," $0, with weekly word caps — 2,000 words/week on Mac/Windows, 1,000/week on iPhone (Help Center adds hard caps: 5,000 desktop, 1,500 iOS, reset Sunday 12am PT). (2) "Flow Pro" is $15/user/month billed monthly. (3) $12/user/month when billed annually, which equals $144/user/year (a 20% discount) — both the per-month annualized figure and the $144/yr total are confirmed. (4) "Flow Enterprise" is Contact Sales. (5) New signups get a 14-day (2-week) Flow Pro free trial with No credit card required. MINOR NUANCE (not a defect): "weekly word caps" is plural and accurate for the metered platforms (desktop + iPhone), but Android is listed as unlimited "for a limited time," so the cap is not universal across every platform. This does not undermine the claim, which correctly characterizes Basic as having weekly word caps. INFERENCE: the $144/yr figure is the direct arithmetic of $12 x 12 and is stated explicitly on the page, so it is a confirmed fact rather than inference. Tried to refute via a second primary source (Help Center) and via search-surfaced third-party reviews (eesel.ai, usevoicy.com); all agree — no contradicting evidence found. Verdict: fully supported.

verdicts.claim: Wispr Flow is cloud-only (no offline mode); marketing claims ~4x faster than typing (220 vs 45 wpm) and reviews cite ~97% accuracy at normal volume.
verdicts.note:
All three components verified, two against primary sources.

CONFIRMED FACTS:
(1) Cloud-only / no offline mode — PRIMARY SOURCE. Wispr Flow's own Help Center states "Flow requires an internet connection for transcription" and its data-controls page states "Transcription always occurs on the cloud" (docs.wisprflow.ai). Multiple independent reviews corroborate: tldv.io — "All transcription happens in the cloud. There is no offline mode"; spokenly.app — "Wispr Flow is cloud-only... There is no on-device mode at any tier." No offline mode exists at any tier. TRUE.

(2) Marketing ~4x faster, 220 vs 45 wpm — PRIMARY SOURCE. The official homepage (wisprflow.ai) states "4x faster than your keyboard" and displays exactly "45 wpm" (keyboard) vs "220 wpm" (Flow). The claim's numbers match the marketing verbatim. TRUE. (Note: 220/45 is technically ~4.9x, but "4x" is Wispr's own rounded marketing figure, so the claim accurately reports what marketing says.)

(3) ~97% accuracy in reviews — SUPPORTED via secondary sources. Independent reviews cite ~97.2%: spokenly.app — "Independent testing puts real-world accuracy around 97.2% on standard English audio"; a cited 30-day independent test also reported 97.2%. tldv.io reports accuracy "consistently exceeding 95%" in a quiet room, approaching 99% with a headset mic. TRUE.

INFERENCE / MINOR NUANCE: The claim's exact qualifier "at normal volume" is not standard phrasing in the sources — reviewers instead specify conditions like "standard English audio," "clean audio," or "quiet room," which is the same spirit (normal/clean speaking conditions) but not verbatim. One outlier (Willow Voice, a competitor) claims only 90% for Wispr Flow, but that is a self-interested comparison; the ~97% figure dominates across neutral sources. This soft spot does not undermine the overall claim.

Sources: https://wisprflow.ai/ (primary, speed claim 220/45 wpm 4x); https://docs.wisprflow.ai/articles/6344532666-android-system-requirements and Wispr help/data-controls pages (primary, cloud-only); https://tldv.io/blog/wisprflow/ ("no offline mode", >95% accuracy); https://spokenly.app/blog/wispr-flow-review (97.2% accuracy, cloud-only, no on-device mode); https://willowvoice.com/blog/wispr-flow-review-voice-dictation (competitor claims 90%, confirms cloud-only).

verdicts.claim: CONFIRMED: All speech recognition is cloud-based; there is no local/offline transcription (Wispr's own words: 'Transcription always occurs on the cloud').
verdicts.note: SUPPORTED. The verbatim quote is genuine and correctly attributed. CONFIRMED FACTS: (1) Wispr Flow's official Data Controls page (https://wisprflow.ai/data-controls) states word-for-word: "Transcription always occurs on the cloud. This is the best way for us to provide accurate, low latency transcription." The quote in the claim matches the primary source exactly. (2) The Security & Compliance FAQ (https://docs.wisprflow.ai/articles/3467817258-security-and-compliance-faq) independently corroborates cloud-only processing: "Wispr's backend must decrypt audio to perform transcription," and explicitly states there is no true end-to-end encryption because the server must see the audio to transcribe it. (3) No official Wispr page mentions any local, on-device, or offline transcription mode; multiple sources confirm the app is unusable without an internet connection, and even "Privacy Mode" is zero-retention CLOUD processing, not local processing. Wikipedia corroborates internet-required operation. ADVERSARIAL FINDING (does NOT refute, but worth flagging): One SEO search snippet claimed "Wispr Flow's voice transcription runs on-device using Whisper." This is FALSE/fabricated — it directly contradicts Wispr's own primary-source statement that transcription always occurs on the cloud, and no credible source supports an on-device Whisper mode. I discarded it. INFERENCE/NUANCE: The claim's framing "All speech recognition is cloud-based" is accurate for the transcription step. The word "always" in Wispr's phrasing is unconditional and covers Privacy Mode too, so the claim's absolute framing ("there is no local/offline transcription") is faithful to the source. The only minor caveat is that Wispr's statement is a marketing/policy assertion of current architecture rather than a technical impossibility, but as a description of the product as it exists, the claim is fully supported by the primary source.

verdicts.claim: CONFIRMED: The architecture is a two-stage pipeline — custom 'voice-first' speech-recognition model(s) for ASR, then fine-tuned Meta Llama models for transcript enhancement (filler removal, punctuation, capitalization, formatting, tone-matching).
verdicts.note:
CORE ARCHITECTURE CONFIRMED, but two claim elements are overstated, so overall = PARTLY.

CONFIRMED (primary source, Baseten case study): (1) Two-stage pipeline — verbatim: "Flow's entire pipeline, from speech recognition models to Llama-based transcript enhancement, runs end-to-end in under 700 milliseconds." (2) Fine-tuned Meta Llama for the second stage — Wispr "chose Llama, a family of open-source LLMs by Meta, as the base for their real-time transcript cleanup step" and "fine-tuned these LLMs." (3) Tone/style-matching is explicitly part of the Llama stage ("I write differently when texting my mom, my wife, and my colleagues"; "matching Flow's output to the user's context and preferences").

WELL-CORROBORATED (Wispr's why-flow/features pages + multiple reviews, though NOT itemized in the Baseten primary source): the Llama cleanup stage removes filler/self-corrections, adds punctuation, capitalizes, and formats lists/paragraphs/emails. Accurate.

OVERSTATEMENT 1 — "voice-first": REFUTED as an official descriptor. The word "voice-first" does NOT appear in the Baseten case study, the wisprflow.ai/why-flow page, or Wikipedia. It surfaces only in third-party marketing/blog titles (e.g., "voice-first productivity"). It is not a primary-source label for the ASR model.

OVERSTATEMENT 2 — "custom" ASR model(s): only PARTLY supported. Wispr says it predates Whisper and is not a Whisper wrapper (proprietary layering is real). But TechCrunch and secondary sources indicate Flow currently runs a SET of ASR models selected per detected language on THIRD-PARTY infra (OpenAI and Meta listed as subprocessors), and that building "its own voice models / personalized ASR" is a STATED FUTURE GOAL, not current state. So "custom" ASR is aspirational/partial rather than a confirmed present-day fact.

INFERENCE vs FACT: The two-stage + fine-tuned-Llama + tone-matching claims are FACTS from a primary source. The "voice-first" and "custom ASR" characterizations are INFERENCE/marketing not backed by primary sources. Net: the claim's spine is true but it mislabels the ASR, so 'partly' not 'supported'.

verdicts.claim: UNCERTAIN/INFERENCE: The ASR stage is a proprietary custom model; whether it is Whisper-derived is NOT confirmed by Wispr. It is plausible only because Wispr's serving vendor Baseten publicly runs an optimized streaming Whisper — but this is inference, not fact.
verdicts.note: Every factual assertion in the claim checks out against primary sources, and its self-labeling as inference/uncertain is correct. CONFIRMED FACTS: (1) Proprietary/custom ASR — Wispr's own engineering post describes building "context-conditioned ASR models (conditioned on speaker qualities, surrounding context, and individual history)" and "the world's best ASR models (context aware, personalized, and code-switched)," language consistent with in-house custom models (wisprflow.ai/post/technical-challenges). (2) Whisper-derivation NOT confirmed by Wispr — across Wispr's Research page, technical-challenges post, and speech-recognition-challenges post, Whisper is never mentioned; neither confirmed nor denied. Wikipedia and Baseten's case study also leave the ASR model unspecified. (3) Baseten is Wispr's serving vendor AND publicly runs an optimized streaming Whisper (Whisper Large V3 Turbo) model server — both independently confirmed (baseten.co case study + baseten.co streaming-Whisper blog). So the inference the claim rests on is genuinely available. ONE IMPRECISION (does not change verdict, and the claim already hedges): the documented Wispr<->Baseten relationship is specifically about the fine-tuned Llama transcript-enhancement stage — the case study states "Baseten powers Flow with low-latency inference on dedicated deployments for these fine-tuned Llama models" and never says Wispr's ASR runs on Baseten or uses Baseten's Whisper (Baseten's public streaming-Whisper names Notion, not Wispr, as the customer). Thus the Baseten->Whisper->Wispr-ASR bridge is weaker than the phrasing hints, but the claim correctly frames it as inference not fact, so it stands. Caveat: one SECONDARY source (getvoibe.com) characterizes the ASR as "built on top of Whisper-class architecture" but also says it does not use OpenAI's Whisper as primary engine — this is a third-party claim, is internally ambiguous ("Whisper-class" != "Whisper-derived"), and is not from Wispr, so it does not upgrade the derivation question to confirmed.

verdicts.claim: End-to-end pipeline latency is under 700ms at p99, using streaming, TensorRT-LLM-optimized Llama, Baseten Chains multi-step inference, and autoscaling GPUs on AWS.
verdicts.note: Adversarial check against primary sources. 5 of the 6 specific technical assertions are directly and precisely confirmed by Baseten's own published Wispr Flow customer case study (a primary source): (1) "Flow's entire pipeline... runs end-to-end in under 700 milliseconds. This latency target isn't for p50 or even p90 – this is the p99 latency" — CONFIRMED, including the p99 qualifier verbatim; (2) "Baseten's TensorRT-LLM engine builder for fine-tuned Llama models" — CONFIRMED (TensorRT-LLM-optimized Llama); (3) "Chains framework for multi-step inference" — CONFIRMED; (4) "access autoscaling GPUs to handle traffic spikes" (scales to zero when idle) — CONFIRMED; (5) "Baseten runs Flow's Llama inference on AWS workload planes" — CONFIRMED (on AWS). Wispr Flow's own engineering post corroborates the sub-700ms budget (ASR <200ms, LLM <200ms, networking <=200ms). The ONE element I could not confirm is "streaming": neither the Baseten case study nor Wispr Flow's technical-challenges post uses the word "streaming" or describes token-by-token/incremental streaming inference. That descriptor is an unsupported embellishment (plausible for a low-latency pipeline but not stated by any source found), so the verbatim claim is not fully supported. Because the load-bearing facts are all confirmed and only the "streaming" qualifier is uncorroborated, the accurate verdict is 'partly' (strongly leaning supported). Note: this claim is impressively well-grounded relative to typical marketing copy — the p99 (not p50/p90) precision is a notable point in its favor and matches the source exactly.
verdicts.source: https://www.baseten.co/resources/customers/wispr-flow/ (primary — Baseten customer case study; confirms 700ms p99, TensorRT-LLM Llama, Chains multi-step inference, autoscaling GPUs, AWS workload planes) ; https://wisprflow.ai/post/technical-challenges (primary — Wispr Flow engineering post; confirms sub-700ms budget split ASR<200ms/LLM<200ms/networking<=200ms; does NOT mention streaming, TensorRT-LLM, Baseten, or AWS by name)

verdicts.claim: Text post-processing uses fine-tuned Llama 3.1 plus proprietary third-party LLM providers; secondary sourcing names OpenAI, Anthropic, and Cerebras (from the NDA-gated subprocessor list).
verdicts.note:
CORE SUBSTANCE SUPPORTED, TWO SPECIFICS FAIL. Confirmed facts: (1) Baseten's official Wispr Flow case study confirms a FINE-TUNED LLAMA model powers the real-time transcript-cleanup/post-processing step ("chose Llama... as the base for their real-time transcript cleanup step... fine-tuned these LLMs"). PRIMARY SOURCE. (2) OpenAI, Anthropic, and Cerebras are named as the text-processing (Polish/formatting) providers, corroborated by multiple secondary sources reading Wispr's own subprocessor docs.

REFUTED / UNSUPPORTED SPECIFIC #1 — the version "3.1": No source I found — not the Baseten case study, not Wispr's engineering posts (wisprflow.ai/post/technical-challenges), not any secondary source — specifies Llama *3.1*. Every primary source says "Llama" (family) WITHOUT a version number. The "3.1" is unsupported fabricated specificity; treat as inference, not fact. Baseten explicitly does not name a version.

MISLEADING SPECIFIC #2 — "(from the NDA-gated subprocessor list)": The provider names (OpenAI/Anthropic/Cerebras) are actually drawn from a PUBLICLY PUBLISHED, self-serve subprocessor list (docs.wisprflow.ai/articles/5375461355-subprocessors-third-party-security, last updated 13 May 2026) — the Medium and Voibe articles read them off it with NO NDA. Secondary sources explicitly state "Nothing about this is hidden. It's all in Wispr Flow's own subprocessor list." A separate NDA gate exists, but it covers the AUTHORITATIVE contractual list (DPA Annex 2) and compliance reports (SOC 2, ISO 27001) via the Trust Center — NOT the provider names, which are public. So attributing the names to "the NDA-gated subprocessor list" mis-sources them.

VERDICT: partly. The gist (fine-tuned Llama for cleanup + third-party OpenAI/Anthropic/Cerebras for text processing) is well-supported by primary + secondary sources, but the "3.1" version is unverifiable/unsupported and the "NDA-gated" sourcing attribution is inaccurate (those names are publicly documented).

verdicts.claim: CONFIRMED: Text is placed at the cursor system-wide via OS Accessibility APIs (required permission on macOS/Windows; Accessibility service on Android; third-party keyboard on iOS), with automatic clipboard fallback if insertion fails.
verdicts.note:
Verdict: PARTLY. The claim bundles four assertions; two are correct, two are wrong or mischaracterized. Grade PARTLY (not CONFIRMED).

CONFIRMED sub-claims:
1. Text is inserted at the cursor across apps ("system-wide") on all platforms. (multiple docs)
2. Android uses the Accessibility Service to insert text: "Flow uses Android's accessibility service to detect when you focus a text field and to insert dictated text into it" (setup-android doc). Accessibility permission IS required on Android.
3. iOS uses a third-party custom keyboard: "Add Wispr Flow as a keyboard on your iPhone to dictate directly into Messages, Mail, Slack..."; iOS text "appears directly in the text field without using the clipboard" (fix-text-not-pasting doc). Confirmed.
4. Clipboard fallback exists on Android: "if insertion can't be verified, Flow shows a copy button on the Flow Bubble" / falls back to clipboard-based insertion / saves to Flow Notes. Confirmed for Android.

REFUTED / MISCHARACTERIZED sub-claims:
A. WINDOWS ACCESSIBILITY PERMISSION IS NOT REQUIRED (directly refutes "required permission on macOS/Windows"). Primary doc: "Flow does not require app-level Microphone or Accessibility permissions on Windows — only system Microphone access matters." Windows has no OS-level "Accessibility permission" grant like macOS/Android. This part is flatly wrong.

B. DESKTOP MECHANISM IS MISCHARACTERIZED. The claim frames Accessibility-API direct insertion as primary and clipboard as a mere "fallback." Primary docs show the opposite on desktop: on Mac & Windows, Flow's PRIMARY insertion method is clipboard paste — it "temporarily uses your clipboard to paste text" via Cmd+V / Ctrl+V (Shift+Insert in some IDE terminals), then restores prior clipboard contents. On macOS the accessibility permission enables the paste/keystroke simulation and context-reading (e.g. "Flow reads IDE context via accessibility APIs"), but the actual text delivery is clipboard paste, not a direct AXUIElement value write. So on desktop clipboard is the default path, not a failure fallback.

Net: The Android and iOS descriptions are accurate. The macOS accessibility-permission requirement is real. But (1) the blanket "Accessibility APIs" insertion mechanism is inaccurate for desktop (it's clipboard paste), and (2) the Windows accessibility-permission requirement is false. Because the "CONFIRMED" claim contains a clear factual error (Windows) plus a mechanism mischaracterization (desktop clipboard-primary), it cannot stand as fully supported. Half-true -> PARTLY. Facts drawn from official Wispr Flow docs (primary source); no engineering blog / source code was located to describe the exact API calls (e.g. AXUIElement, UI Automation), so the low-level "OS Accessibility APIs" implementation detail is an inference, not a documented fact.

verdicts.claim: Wispr Flow context awareness reads limited text near the cursor + active-app/website identity + app metadata (and code identifiers / recent chat messages) and uploads it to the cloud to improve accuracy and formatting; password fields are excluded; screenshot capture exists but is opt-in.
verdicts.note:
All eight sub-claims confirmed against Wispr Flow's own primary documentation.

CONFIRMED FACTS (verbatim from docs.wisprflow.ai + wisprflow.ai):
1. Limited text near cursor: "Flow reads a limited amount of text near your cursor and identifies your active app." (Context Awareness doc)
2. Active app + website identity: "For browser-based apps, Flow identifies the specific website, so Gmail, Google Docs, and other web apps are recognized individually." (Context Awareness doc)
3. App metadata: "relevant context (nearby text, proper nouns extracted from on-screen content, app metadata, and — in coding environments — variable names and file names) is sent to Wispr." (Context Awareness doc)
4. Code identifiers: "in coding environments — variable names and file names"; "Flow remembers file names seen in Cursor, Windsurf, and VS Code across dictation sessions." (Context Awareness doc)
5. Recent chat messages: "For chat apps, recent conversation messages may also be included." (Context Awareness doc)
6. Cloud upload to improve accuracy/formatting: "During an active dictation session, relevant context...is sent to Wispr to improve transcription accuracy." The accessibility-text component "Reads text from the active application's accessibility tree to improve AI formatting." (Context Awareness doc + Security & Compliance FAQ)
7. Password fields excluded: "Password field contents are never read or included in context data." (Context Awareness doc)
8. Screenshot capture exists + opt-in: Context Awareness has two separately-toggled parts — Accessibility-text context (default ON) and "Screen OCR (default off): Captures a full-display screenshot to extract proper nouns. This is opt-in." (Security & Compliance FAQ)

ADVERSARIAL / CAVEATS (inference, not refutation):
- The main Context Awareness page does NOT mention screenshots at all; the screenshot/opt-in detail lives on a separate Wispr page (Security & Compliance FAQ). It still holds because it is Wispr's own official doc, but the claim bundles two docs' contents.
- Password-field exclusion carries a documented edge case: on Mac, only standard macOS password fields are auto-detected; custom/non-standard password fields "may not be detected and could be read like normal text fields." This qualifies but does not contradict the stated exclusion policy.
- Real-world context: this Context Awareness/screenshot behavior became public via a late-2025 privacy incident (a user found screenshots being uploaded before clear disclosure; Wispr initially banned the user, then the CTO apologized and the policy/docs were updated). The current documented behavior matches the claim; the claim describes the post-incident documented state.

verdicts.claim: CONFIRMED: Custom vocabulary is handled via a user-editable personal Dictionary and automatic learning from typed corrections; 100+ languages with code-switching.
verdicts.note: Three of the four sub-claims are confirmed by Wispr Flow primary sources; the fourth (code-switching) is real but overstated as a settled feature. FACTS (confirmed by official docs): (1) User-editable personal Dictionary — the help doc states it is "fully user-editable," letting users manually add words and create replacement rules for persistent misspellings [https://docs.wisprflow.ai/articles/4052411709-teach-flow-your-words-with-the-dictionary]. (2) Automatic learning from typed corrections — same doc: "When enabled in Settings > System > Extras > Auto-add to dictionary, Flow learns words it doesn't recognize from your corrections"; the features page adds "When you correct a spelling, Flow adds it automatically to your personal dictionary" [https://wisprflow.ai/features]. CAVEAT (inference-adjacent, from docs): auto-learning is an opt-in setting, not on by default, and only adds names/uncommon proper nouns, marking them with a sparkle icon — so "automatic" is accurate but conditional. (3) 100+ languages — CONFIRMED by the official research page ("natural, accurate voice-to-text in 100+ languages") and the features page ("100+ languages") [https://wisprflow.ai/research/supporting-languages, https://wisprflow.ai/features]. WEAK LINK (why not fully supported): code-switching. The official research page frames it as "ongoing code-mixing experiments" (Hinglish pilot, "paving the way for better mixed-language support") — i.e., in-development, not a mature confirmed feature [https://wisprflow.ai/research/supporting-languages]. The multi-language help doc describes code-switching as explicitly LIMITED: "Flow works best when you speak primarily in one language with occasional words from another, rather than alternating sentence by sentence," and notes Chinese-English mixing is unreliable [https://docs.wisprflow.ai/articles/3191899797-use-flow-with-multiple-languages]. The features page does not mention code-switching at all. So code-switching EXISTS (real for Hinglish-type pairs with auto-detect) but is experimental/constrained, not the settled capability the flat "CONFIRMED" implies. Net: the dictionary + auto-learning + 100+ languages portions are supported; bundling "code-switching" under the same unqualified CONFIRMED label overstates a partial/experimental feature, so the overall claim is partly true.

verdicts.claim: CONFIRMED: Privacy is user-controllable (Privacy Mode + Cloud Sync = zero retention), but retention/training-use is ON by default for non-HIPAA users; encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest; the backend must decrypt audio to transcribe (no true end-to-end encryption).
verdicts.note:
Every load-bearing sub-claim is confirmed by Wispr Flow's own primary sources (official site + Help Center). I tried to refute it and could not.

CONFIRMED FACTS (quoted from official sources):
1. User-controllable zero retention: "Enable Privacy Mode and disable Cloud Sync for zero data retention. This means no dictation data used for training, and no audio or transcripts stored on our servers" (wisprflow.ai/privacy; data-controls). Privacy Mode governs training; Cloud Sync governs server storage.
2. Training/retention ON by default: "If you do not have Privacy Mode enabled, your dictation data may be used to evaluate, train, and improve Wispr features and AI models" (data-controls). Privacy Mode is opt-in (off by default) and Cloud Sync is enabled by default, so retention + training-use are the default state for a normal consumer account.
3. Encryption in transit: "All confidential data in transit is encrypted using TLS 1.2 or higher with forward-secrecy cipher suites" (security-and-compliance-faq) — exactly matches "TLS 1.2+".
4. Encryption at rest: "Stored data is encrypted at rest using AES-256 at the database and object-storage layers" backed by FIPS 140-2 HSM key management (security-and-compliance-faq).
5. No true E2EE: "Wispr's backend must decrypt audio to perform transcription"; under Zero Data Retention the protection is architectural (content not persisted) rather than cryptographic (security-and-compliance-faq). Verbatim match to the claim.

ONE MINOR IMPRECISION (does not change verdict, hence 'supported' not 'partly'): The claim frames the default-on exception as "non-HIPAA users," implying a clean HIPAA vs non-HIPAA split. The HIPAA/ZDR doc actually states Privacy Mode is locked ON when EITHER the user has an active HIPAA BAA OR an enterprise enforces Zero Data Retention. So HIPAA is one covered case, but enterprise-ZDR-enforced (non-HIPAA) users are also locked on. The claim's core point — that ordinary consumer/non-exempt users have retention/training on by default — is correct; the HIPAA label is slightly narrow but not wrong.

INFERENCE (not asserted by claim, flagged for transparency): "Cloud Sync enabled by default" is stated in the docs; combined with Privacy Mode being opt-in, this is why the default = retention + training. SOC 2 Type II (Feb-May 2025, ACCORP Partners) corroborates the encryption controls but is not part of the claim.

Note: several docs.wisprflow.ai article URLs returned 404 to direct WebFetch, but their content was retrievable and consistent via search snippets and the fetchable wisprflow.ai marketing pages, so corroboration is multi-source, not single-page.

verdicts.claim: whisper-local (drajb/whisper-local, MIT, Python) is the closest existing offline Wispr Flow clone: it already ships AMD ROCm-on-Windows GPU setup, SendInput injection, optional Ollama LLM polish, and a voice-command config.
verdicts.note:
CONFIRMED FACTS (all verified against primary sources, not just the repo's marketing prose):

(1) Identity/license/language — GitHub REST API for the repo returns license spdx_id=MIT, language=Python, default branch=master, description "Free, open-source, 100% offline AI dictation for Windows & macOS. Wispr Flow / Dragon alternative." So "drajb/whisper-local, MIT, Python" is exactly right. (Note it is a small, new project: created 2026-05-06, ~5 stars, last push 2026-07-02.)

(2) AMD ROCm-on-Windows — CONFIRMED and this is the strongest part of the claim, which I tried hardest to refute (ROCm is historically Linux-only). docs/gpu-setup.md has a dedicated "AMD — RDNA 2+ (ROCm)" section that is unambiguously NATIVE WINDOWS, not WSL/Linux: it installs the Windows "HIP SDK 7.1" installer, references Windows path C:\Program Files\AMD\ROCm\7.1\bin, uses PowerShell + win_amd64.whl wheels (rocm_sdk_core-7.2.0...win_amd64.whl), and requires the AMD Adrenalin 26.1.1 Windows driver. It reuses device: cuda as the config key for AMD (HIP maps onto the CUDA path via CTranslate2). RDNA1 (RX 5000) is also covered.

(3) SendInput injection — CONFIRMED at source: src/whisper_key/platform/windows/keyboard.py line 103 literally calls user32.SendInput(n, array, ctypes.sizeof(INPUT)) with full ctypes INPUT/KEYBDINPUT structs. README tech stack lists "Windows-only: ... ctypes SendInput".

(4) Optional Ollama LLM polish — CONFIRMED: text_postprocess.py has _ollama_polish() posting to http://localhost:11434, gated behind config ollama.enabled (default False), i.e. genuinely optional. README describes an Ollama-backed "AI rephrase / transforms" feature.

(5) Voice-command config — CONFIRMED: voice_commands.py loads commands.yaml (via ruamel.yaml) from user app-data, with each command a trigger mapped to exactly one of run/hotkey/type/rephrase.

INFERENCE / CAVEATS (not refutations): The superlative "closest existing offline Wispr Flow clone" is a comparative judgment no single source can prove; the repo self-describes as "a free, open-source, fully offline alternative to Wispr Flow, Dragon, and Otter" and includes a feature-parity table vs Wispr Flow, which supports the spirit but not the "closest of all" ranking. Minor precision points on the ROCm-on-Windows path: it is a MANUAL setup (DLL rename hipblas.dll fix, or pip-installing a third-party CTranslate2 ROCm wheel from PinW/ctranslate2-rocm-wheels), not the "one-press auto-install" flow — that auto-detect/one-press install is demonstrated mainly for NVIDIA/CUDA. So AMD ROCm-on-Windows is genuinely shipped and documented, but "setup" here means a documented manual procedure relying on an external community wheel, not a turnkey installer. None of this contradicts the claim's wording ("ships AMD ROCm-on-Windows GPU setup"). Every concrete, falsifiable attribute in the claim is accurate; verdict supported.

verdicts.claim: For AMD on Windows there are two proven routes: (a) whisper.cpp GGML Vulkan backend (-DGGML_VULKAN=ON) as Vibe uses — cross-vendor, but you must compile from source since official Windows releases omit Vulkan; or (b) Const-me/Whisper's DirectCompute DLL (MPL-2.0) which runs on any DirectX 11 GPU incl. Radeon with zero CUDA/ROCm dependency.
verdicts.note:
Every load-bearing element verified against primary sources.

ROUTE (a) — whisper.cpp GGML Vulkan:
- CONFIRMED cross-vendor: the Vulkan backend runs on NVIDIA/AMD/Intel/ARM/Qualcomm GPUs with no vendor-specific code (https://github.com/ggml-org/whisper.cpp).
- CONFIRMED Vibe uses it: Vibe's README states it is "Optimized for Nvidia / AMD / Intel GPUs! (Vulkan/CoreML)" (https://github.com/thewh1teagle/vibe).
- CONFIRMED official Windows releases omit Vulkan: issue #3673 states "The Windows release binaries (v1.8.3) include CPU and CUDA builds but no Vulkan build. Users with Intel Arc or AMD GPUs have no GPU-accelerated option without building from source" (https://github.com/ggml-org/whisper.cpp/issues/3673). So compiling from source for Vulkan on Windows is accurate.
- MINOR FLAG NOTATION NITPICK (not an error): the claim writes -DGGML_VULKAN=ON; the official whisper.cpp README uses -DGGML_VULKAN=1 (cmake -B build -DGGML_VULKAN=1). In CMake, ON and 1 are equivalent truthy boolean values, so both compile identically — cosmetic difference only, does not change correctness.

ROUTE (b) — Const-me/Whisper:
- CONFIRMED DirectCompute: README states "Vendor-agnostic GPGPU based on DirectCompute; another name for that technology is 'compute shaders in Direct3D 11'" (https://github.com/Const-me/Whisper).
- CONFIRMED license MPL-2.0: the repository's stated license is MPL-2.0 (Mozilla Public License 2.0). (One search snippet mentioned "MPL-2.0 / MIT," but the repo's declared project license is MPL-2.0, matching the claim.)
- CONFIRMED runs on any DX11 GPU incl. Radeon: "The library requires a Direct3D 11.0 capable GPU, which in 2023 simply means 'any hardware GPU'"; tested on AMD Radeon Vega 7/8 iGPUs among others.
- CONFIRMED zero CUDA/ROCm dependency: it is a Windows DirectCompute implementation and requires neither CUDA nor ROCm.

INFERENCE vs FACT: The individual technical facts (backends, flags, licenses, GPU support, release contents) are all directly sourced. The word "proven" is the claim's editorializing — but both routes are demonstrably real, documented, and in active use (Vibe ships Vulkan; Const-me is a widely used DX11 tool), so it is a fair characterization. No element of the claim was refuted.

verdicts.claim: Claim 20: The Wispr Flow feature to replicate is a two-stage pipeline (STT -> context-aware LLM reformat -> paste); the reusable LOCAL equivalent is faster-whisper/whisper.cpp for STT plus an optional local LLM (Ollama) 'polish' pass fed the active-app name/context, keeping all audio on-device.
verdicts.note:
Every component verified against primary sources; I tried to refute it and could not.

WISPR ARCHITECTURE (confirmed fact, primary sources):
- Two-stage pipeline STT -> LLM reformat -> insert-at-cursor is confirmed by Wispr's OWN engineering blog (wisprflow.ai/post/technical-challenges): "E2E ASR inference in <200ms, E2E LLM inference in <200ms," and "Personalized LLMs with token level formatting control."
- Baseten (Wispr's LLM-inference vendor) states the flow is "speech input -> transcription -> Llama-based formatting -> final output"; CTO Sahaj Garg confirms they fine-tune Meta's Llama for the cleanup step, generating "100+ tokens in under 250ms" (baseten.co/resources/customers/wispr-flow/).
- Active-app context IS fed to formatting: Wispr's Context Awareness docs (docs.wisprflow.ai/articles/4678293671) confirm Flow "identifies your active app and adapts transcription accuracy, style, and formatting" and "reads a limited amount of text near your cursor." The tech blog cites "current app context" as a personalization input.

LOCAL EQUIVALENT (confirmed fact, not just inference):
- faster-whisper (github.com/SYSTRAN/faster-whisper) and whisper.cpp (github.com/ggml-org/whisper.cpp) are established on-device STT engines.
- Ollama runs local LLMs (Llama/Gemma/Mistral) on-device with data never leaving the machine.
- The exact stack described ALREADY EXISTS as shipping tools: github.com/drajb/whisper-local uses faster-whisper for STT + optional Ollama pass for "punctuation and capitalisation polish ... Off by default and fully local," detects the foreground/active window (app_rules.yaml), and pledges "zero network traffic." FreeFlow (github.com/zachlatta/freeflow) and Murmur implement the same Whisper + Ollama + active-app-context pattern.

NUANCE (does not refute): "keeping all audio on-device" applies to the LOCAL replica, NOT to Wispr Flow itself. Wispr's own data-controls page states transcription "always occurs on the cloud" -- Wispr audio is NOT on-device. The claim is careful to attribute on-device only to the local equivalent, so it is accurate as written. Minor redundancy: faster-whisper and whisper.cpp are alternatives to each other (pick one), but offering both as options is correct.
